name: Laxmangarh, the home village
certaintyNote: Laxmangarh is an invented village, but the narrator places it in the real Gaya district of Bihar, in the impoverished Ganges belt he calls the Darkness. Sited at the city of Gaya as a representative anchor for that countryside.
note: The narrator is born into a landless family in a village he calls Laxmangarh, in the poorest stretch of rural Bihar. His father pulls a rickshaw; the boy is taken out of school to work and left with no name of his own beyond Balram. Everything that follows is his escape from this place, which he treats less as a dot on the map than as a whole condition of life.

sources.ref: The White Tiger (Adiga novel), Wikipedia, on the Gaya-district village setting and the theme of the Darkness
Gaya, India, Wikipedia, for the district coordinates on the Ganges plain

name: The Ganges at Varanasi
certaintyNote: The narrator names the Ganges and the holy city on its banks. Sited at the Varanasi riverfront, the classic place of cremation on the river.
note: A funeral takes the narrator to the sacred river, where the dead are burned at the water's edge. He treats the holy Ganges with open scorn, reading its black mud and half-burnt bodies as a fitting emblem of the Darkness he means to escape rather than a source of grace.

sources.ref: The White Tiger (Adiga novel), Wikipedia, on the Ganges and the narrator's view of it
Varanasi, Wikipedia, for the riverfront coordinates on the Ganges

name: The coal town of Dhanbad
certaintyNote: Dhanbad is named in the novel. Sited at the city centre of the coal-mining hub, historically part of the wider Bihar region.
note: Sent to earn money, the young narrator works in a teashop in Dhanbad, the coal-mining town in what is now Jharkhand. Wiping tables and eavesdropping, he studies the drivers and businessmen around him and decides that learning to handle a car is his one way out of the servant class.

sources.ref: The White Tiger (Adiga novel), Wikipedia, on the narrator's move to work in Dhanbad
Dhanbad, Wikipedia, for the coordinates of the coal-mining city

name: The master's world, Gurgaon
certaintyNote: Gurgaon, the satellite city southwest of Delhi, is named in the novel; the specific apartment tower is invented. Sited at the Gurgaon city centre as a representative point.
note: Hired as a driver, the narrator follows his employers to a gleaming apartment block in Gurgaon, the mall-and-tower boomtown outside Delhi. He lives below stairs, ferries the family between shopping centres and shady errands, and watches a world of money he serves but cannot enter, a resentment that slowly hardens.

sources.ref: The White Tiger (Adiga novel), Wikipedia, on the narrator's service in Delhi and Gurgaon
Gurgaon, Wikipedia, for the coordinates of the Delhi satellite city

name: Connaught Place, New Delhi
certaintyNote: Connaught Place is named among the Delhi landmarks the narrator drives through. Sited at the colonnaded central circle of New Delhi.
note: Waiting on his employers, the narrator idles among the traffic and beggars of the capital's grand central circle, watching the gulf between the people in the back seat and the men who drive them. Delhi is where his resentment hardens into a plan, and where the single violent act that frees him takes shape.

sources.ref: The White Tiger (Adiga novel), Wikipedia, on the Delhi setting where the plot turns
Connaught Place, New Delhi, Wikipedia, for the coordinates of the central circle

name: Bangalore, the new master
certaintyNote: Bangalore is named as the narrator's endpoint. Sited at the city centre of the southern technology capital.
note: Blood money and a stolen identity carry the narrator south to the technology capital, where he sets up a taxi firm ferrying call-centre workers through the night. He ends the book as one of the entrepreneurs he once served, telling his whole story as a set of night letters written from an office in the city of the future.

sources.ref: The White Tiger (Adiga novel), Wikipedia, on the Bangalore ending and the narrator's taxi business
Bangalore, Wikipedia, for the coordinates of the technology capital
